items.q: What is the difference between strength and power?
items.correct: Strength is maximum force; power is force times speed of movement.
items.random_wrong: Strength is for the upper body, power for the lower.
items.why: Students conflate strength and power. A strong person can lift heavy slowly; a powerful athlete generates force QUICKLY (sprinter, shot-putter). Power requires both force and velocity.

items.q: Which energy system fuels a marathon?
items.why: Students associate hard exercise with anaerobic systems. Long-duration steady-state work uses AEROBIC metabolism (oxygen + carbs/fats) - efficient but slower to ramp up. Anaerobic systems dominate brief intense efforts.

items.q: How long does the ATP-PC system last at maximum intensity?
items.why: Students overestimate the ATP-PC system. It provides instant energy but stores deplete in ~10 seconds (one sprint, one heavy lift). After that, glycolysis or aerobic systems take over.

items.q: OBLA conventionally refers to blood lactate of:
items.why: Correct answer: ~4 mmol L⁻¹. “~1 mmol L⁻¹” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An elite endurance athlete's lactate threshold typically occurs at:
items.why: It's ~85% of VO₂max. “~50% of VO₂max” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If LT corresponds to 75% of VO₂max = 60 mL kg⁻¹ min⁻¹, calculate LT VO₂ (mL kg⁻¹ min⁻¹).
items.why: The real figure is 45. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
